text: Confucius compared inadequate self-cultivation to an unfinished earthwork: "If I stop when the work is one basketful short of completion, it is I who stop." The metaphor is architectural — a mound not quite complete remains a hole. Progress not consolidated is progress lost. This anticipates what psychologists call the completion effect: unfinished tasks linger in working memory, consuming attentional resources until resolved. The Confucian student was expected to carry each lesson to genuine closure — not to the end of a session but to the point where the material could be reproduced without the teacher present.
question: What does Confucius's earthwork metaphor illustrate about self-cultivation?

options.text: That effort must be distributed evenly across all tasks
That physical labor builds the discipline needed for study
That progress not consolidated to completion is progress lost
That teachers should set high and exacting standards
